speaker: interviewer
text: The date is May 6th, 1996. The interview is with survivor Sydney Schwimmer. And this is tape number two. When we left off the last tape, you were talking about your student exemption from military service. Can you just tell us briefly about where you went from the gymnasium, where your next step in your education was?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I have graduated the gymnasium, in 1936 in June the 16, I went to Brno, to medical school. Why did I choose Brno? This was the second largest city in Czechoslovakia. Brno had a medical school where was a lot of privileges which I could have there since my father couldn't afford to send me to university, despite the university was completely free, but over there to live there someplace and that. Brno had a very, very large community, Jewish community, and very favorable to students who wanted to study. Brno was a town where a lot of pretty wealthy Jews lived there. And in their community, they made two things. Number one, they had a special building which for Jewish students or for orphans who cannot afford, they can live there. Now, there was three, four, five in a room.

speaker: interviewer
text: But beside this, Brno had something else which was very familiar. Brno had what they call it a [NON-ENGLISH]. What's [NON-ENGLISH]? [NON-ENGLISH] is a free table. [NON-ENGLISH] meant that everyone who comes there can eat lunch [? regardless. ?] They don't ask questions. No questions being asked. As a matter of fact, this [NON-ENGLISH] what you call it, was right in the same building where this dormitory was for students.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, on Brno, in the street [INAUDIBLE], number 31, this was this building, a five, six-story building. It was an orphanage home for people who [INAUDIBLE]. And there was a studentheim and was also [NON-ENGLISH] also there. And that quite a big one, because not only students ate there from who went to university who couldn't afford, but also poor people. Everyone to come there to eat lunch had a free lunch. Now, coming in a town, no questions were asked to the line and went in. Wouldn't say that lunch was the best, but it was a little.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now if you have something like this, you have a place where to sleep and you have a place where to eat, so already it's not-- I didn't have to, have to pay tuition. This was convenient for a student who couldn't afford, especially me. And so I went to Brno and lived there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Beside that, there were also a lot of privileges. For instance, the Czech regime gave three times a year a free ticket to go home on Christmas and other holidays and also in the summer. You could go free by train. Naturally, those people who couldn't afford. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was Brno from your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brno from Dolha is quite a-- quite-- it's quite long. It's about 12, 15 hours of travel. It's, it's quite a distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: By train?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train.

speaker: interviewer
text: By train, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's quite a big, big distance, but free. Now also this studentheim also was under the supervision of a certain-- I mean, you couldn't just come there and not to study. You had to prove every semester that you passed your examination in order to be-- to live there. I mean, they didn't want to accept people who just will come in-- and students-- and will not do their job, in other words. So they had to bring from the university every half year, whatever it was, that, that you are enrolled and you are still-- you made your examinations.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what is significant is here. Over there were not students only from the Carpatho-Russia like who were poor, like I am. There were also students from Hungary, and from Poland, and from other. Why from Hungary? In Hungary, as you know, was numerus clausus, and in Poland also. Those who were studying there engineering, not only medical school, anything they were studying there [INAUDIBLE] also.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say in that studentenheim, they call it studentenhelm, there were approximately, at one time, 60, 80, up to 100 students living four in a room, five in a room, depending what. We had two floors there. And this was a very convenient. And this was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in Brno, I started to go university. And started-- this was a Czech university. They had another university also in Brno, a technical, which was German. So it was very convenient.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the first year, I made my first examinations in physics and biology. The second year, I learned anatomy. I mean, the curriculum-- I'm not going to describe it to you. I still have some papers about the documents. And so this was very, very convenient to go there. Now, I was a boy, 20, 21 years old-- still started to think of something. Well, I do not want to live in a small place like Dolha. And I would like to emigrate someplace. It is small. Jews are a minority. The [INAUDIBLE] despite its democracy. But Jews are a minority. And also already in 1937, '38, you heard a lot about Hitler, what's going on in Germany, the Anschluss in Austria, 1938. I'm not going to describe historical facts. So I, myself, was considering to emigrate. However, there were two obstacles. The biggest obstacle was that there's during the Czech regime, I was under military-- what you call it-- rule. I didn't serve my military. If I serve my military, I can get a passport. Not being I was exempt, I couldn't serve. So this was for, the time being, impossible. However, I went already to organizations like [INAUDIBLE] and other Zionist organization to see what could be done to go to Palestine. And also went-- was inquiring about the American Council, how I can go there. Naturally, a lot of obstacles. And I kind of describe it here. To America to go out in those days was very difficult. You had to wait on a quota. And the quota was filled. However, you had to have an affidavit. I didn't have no one in the United States. But I was thinking very hardly what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how long would medical school have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this medical school was, of course, and others, if you studied very well, it was a six year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Six Years.

speaker: interviewee
text: In other words, at the first three years, you had to study physics, biology, anatomy, histology, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] chemistry, and one more subject I forgot already. And then you went into other studies for that six years. After the six years, you did get a doctor degree. And you became a doctor and went in.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far did you get--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went--

speaker: interviewer
text: In that until things were interrupted?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll, I'll, I'll come to this subject.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll come to this subject. So I was in my second year, it was 1938 already. It was the Anschluss. There was now, right now, starting considering how to emigrate, how to go out from here. But the obstacles was there that I don't have-- didn't have where to go out to. And beside this, I was under military-- what you call it-- [NON-ENGLISH], Czech. And before you want to apply for a passport, you must have your status cleared. And the Czechs wouldn't let out anyone at then-- at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now comes 1938. You know about Anschluss? With Anschluss, and we heard about Hitler. Everyone was starting to think about emigrating, including myself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: 1938 started with the Sudetenland about Hitler-- what's going to happen. Final things before we are going further-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with England made an-- before they made their peace in 1938 in October, there was a mobilization. The Czech mobilized. And I was mobilized for a simple reason, because I didn't serve yet. But from that point on, Sudeten was given-- Chamberlain was given to, to Germany. And a part of Czechoslovakia, mostly the southern part of the Carpathian, was also annexed to Hungary-- not the whole Carpathian. But the town of Dolha, where I belonged, wasn't an annexed to [INAUDIBLE]. But exile was also [? enacted. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So here I am in Brno, finding myself. My town is still there, Czechoslovakia, where my friends who are studying there also-- from Beregsaz, from Muncasz are already in Hungary. Hungary, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them been back. I didn't go back. I didn't go. In 1938, after the annexation of the Sudeten, it became really very, very bad in Czechoslovakia. The Czechoslovakia regime already-- now they were anti-Semite. It's already-- the whole army became-- didn't have to go already. I, I was already released from the army. I could go out. I was starting very hard to think, but there was no place where to go. It's impossible to get a passport. And it's impossible. There's no place. I went to the American Council in Prague, started to, wanted to register. But you have to have an affidavit before. Before that, you could register to be in the [? quota ?] without affidavit. Now you have to have affidavit for America. I didn't have anyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, didn't know what to do, and studied further. Now we are coming to a point. I'm going to skip very-- because there's too much talk about these two years, '37, '38. We're coming to '39.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on March the 15th, on a Wednesday morning, 1939, we were in that studentenheim. Early in the morning around 6:00, we woke up. And they don't let out. An SS man was starting right near the door. We are living in a six story building. The building is still today. And as we never heard. We never knew. Hitler marched into Czechoslovakia on the 15th of March, 1939. And the, the minute they came into Brno, what they did is the following. They occupied all these Jewish institutions. [INAUDIBLE] was also considered a Jewish institution. And they didn't let out no one from this building. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we saw a lot of SS men. We didn't realize no one knew about it. It came just suddenly. In Prague, they came in during the night. But in Brno, they came in. And they occupied almost every Jewish institution, the Jewish community, and so on and so on. We were students there somewhere 24, 25. Some were already in their sixth year. We didn't know what to do. It took quite a long time. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they specifically looking for Jewish students at that time, or just anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were not looking. They were Jewish institutions--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish institution. This was also a Jewish institution. Because the [INAUDIBLE] were only a Jewish. The whole building was a Jewish institution.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the Jewish community. There were other also institutions-- I mean buildings-- belong to the, to Jewish institution. But this was specifically. And we didn't realize. We didn't know what, what it is, what it means. But we heard already the news. Well, the same time already, the Czech papers turned completely different. They were controlled by the Germans-- naturally, all kinds of articles about Jews and so on. It took quite a time until they let us out from there, from the institution. From that institution.

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] Go ahead. Yeah, I don't know where it's coming from. Go ahead. We'll, we'll try to talk over the, the noise.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what's happening? The following-- it took quite a few hours. Maybe 10:00 or so, they let out everyone. Naturally, with [INAUDIBLE], who was, who wasn't, and so on. And we went back to the university. Here comes the point. As we come to university, while we're at this building [INAUDIBLE] faculty, in the front of the building was a big sign there-- don't exactly [INAUDIBLE]. But I'm giving-- paraphrasing-- that Jews cannot attend any longer the university. It didn't say expelled, but cannot attend anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just couldn't attend.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, no one was standing there to check who is a Jew and it was not a Jew, but-- and so we went to our classes. It was a turmoil already, as you know. And no one knew what to do and how. But we have seen what's happening here, papers starting right. So we came to the class. And still, I mean, they didn't say it was the professor. But we understood [? till ?] there's a sign like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: A day later or so, everyone when he came who was Jewish did get a letter being expelled from the university. Can you imagine that? People who studied there for six years before the doctorate or so. This was on the next day, 13 March to 16. So we didn't go there. So this was then. No. This was the end of being a student. So I have that. In other words, I made my two and a half years, which I was in Brno 1937, 1938, to 1939, the first semester. I made five semesters. And I had my examinations. What's to do next step? But in the meantime, Hungary annexed the entire Carpathian. All the Carpatho-Russian and Czechs didn't exist anymore. It was made that protectorate of Czechoslovakia. Slovakia became a state by itself. [INAUDIBLE]. This happened on the 15th of March, 1939. Now, I find myself in a protectorate here, where Hitler is. My parents and my family finds themselves in Hungary. Now, was your mother alive at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother died in 1937 at home. She's buried in Dolha. She died of a natural death. And she's buried in Dolha. She was sick. She died at the age of 50.

speaker: interviewer
text: So many students tried-- attempted, attempted to go back home, to go to Hungary. They're going to continue their studies in Hungary, in Budapest, whatever it is. Many other students went different places. And many students remained there. I'm speaking about Jewish students, not Czech or others nationality.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we became, became a protectorate. And my hometown is now Hungary. I really didn't know too much was in Hungary. But borders started to be closed little by little. In the beginning, some people went away. It wasn't such a big deal. And they went back to Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: I chose, for the time being, to stay in Brno. Why? What am I going to do I come back to Dolha? What am I going to in small town? What I'm going to do? It's Hungary. I'll see what's going to be in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And beside this I was occupied with [BACKGROUND NOISE] emigration. I want to go out. I want to get out regardless of-- what.

speaker: interviewer
text: Want to stop? OK. OK, please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Second day, on March 16, 1944, we came to university-- big sign, all Jews expelled. So what are we doing to do? What I'm going to do from now? Jews expelled from university-- not only from medicals, from all over. But the sign where we went was on the university doors and the main entrance, a big sign by the new regime. I don't know exactly how it-- but the content was Jews were expelled.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't know what to do. I mean expelled cannot do, cannot help. But still I was thinking, I mean, my own, what I'm going to do here? So I didn't go back to Hungary. Hungary was annexed with-- I mean, Carpathian were annexed to Hungary. We didn't know too much about Hungary. Many students took the train and went back home and packed up themselves, were going to study in Hungary. If something will happen there-- I mean, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: But now, we still are in the studenteneheim, in the dormitory. Most of the students are there. And the [INAUDIBLE] still exist, bigger and bigger lines every day. Come the second day, everyone starts to think what to do. So in my case, I said to myself the following, I want to emigrate. I want to get out from here somewhere, but I'm not going to go to back to Hungary. Because I want to go either to Palestine, either to United States, or anyplace else where I can go. I had with me already some sort of a passport. And this passport I received before, about two months ago, that I want to go for a summer, for vacation to France, to study French language. This was during the transit between the protectorate and still was Czechoslovakia. Now, a Czech passport, but it said only to go to France, not to other states. But at least it was a passport. And I was trying to work out was immigration, starting to go to offices. I went to Prague. In the meantime, I received also an affidavit from America, from the United States. I had some family. I was tutoring their children when I still was going to the gymnasium in [INAUDIBLE] by the name Neufeld. And they lived here. I wrote to them, begged them already, [? going come ?] for a year or two. I didn't realize they were not citizens, so they couldn't send me an affidavit. But somehow, they did manage. They asked an insurance man by the name of Shubert. And he did send me an affidavit. And I went to the consulate. I registered in Prague myself to going to the United States. Naturally, [INAUDIBLE], that it was almost impossible to go. Because the quota was so high, I had to wait for years. And you couldn't do nothing. There were many who were registered much before with priorities. But still, I have something at least that maybe some chance. Also, in the meantime, I was seeing what could be there that was could be something to emigrate to go anyplace, if I go maybe to Palestine. But to Palestine, as you know from history, there were only certificates given out, about 1,500 a year for the entire world. It was almost impossible. I mean, organizations, some head of them, are going.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what's happening now in Brno? All Jewish stores were closed, [? all businesses ?] like this. One of the most important things what happened in Jew-- in Brno was large synagogue, one of the largest, very large, an old one. And in this synagogue was very well-equipped with everything, but a big one.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Jews used to come there. And this was mostly reformed Jews from Prague and Brno. In Brno, a lot of very wealthy Jews lived there. They are mostly manufacturing of textiles. And we wake up in the morning, Friday morning, still guards were there. But they are allowed to go in in this building, only those who belong there. We had a like a identification who belongs to that building. And for those, they allowed, not a stranger, a SS-- in other words, in, in German uniforms. We hear that this synagogue is burning. We are curious and go out. I go to the synagogue. It was not far. As a matter of fact, this street they called it Synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I go there. And many-- a few other [INAUDIBLE]. And we see big synagogue is burning. What happened is the following, that the night, they burn-- they start to burn the synagogue. How would this synagogue found itself on a place where among them were houses, big houses, factories?

speaker: interviewee
text: And German guards were standing there. And the firemen came to make out the fire, but didn't allow. They already were protecting there that the certain other houses shouldn't catch on also fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was witnessing, myself, this big, great synagogue in Brno. At least 3,000 people were sitting there. I went many times where one of the most famous cantors was by the name of [INAUDIBLE]. This was burned down. I don't know how it burned. Burned down to ashes.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, I was there about a few years ago. Now there is nothing. It's only some sort of a garage place or a-- what, I don't know. I have some picture of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was witnessing, myself, the big synagogue. And large demonstration started against Jews. And Brno, the population at that time at least of 300,000. Jews didn't' left too many. Jews about, I would say, 8,000, 10,000, but demonstrations every day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you could see SS people and a lot of collaborators. In [? Jews ?] lived-- in Brno lived a lot of Germans also, collaborators. You could see in the [? white socks ?] going, singing in the streets, and big, big demonstrations, all kinds of slogans. And came a Friday. Next day, we were on the 7th-- this was on the 17th of, of March, when the synagogue was burned. And at that time, they gave our orders on the radio with a lot of brochures that Jews shouldn't get out from their houses, should remain confined to their houses. We didn't know what the reason, but assumed-- actually, it was suppose Hitler was supposed to come to Brno to the [? second ?]. He was in Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Hitler came there. I, being considered myself, I'm not a citizen there any longer. I'm [INAUDIBLE]. Who's going to tell me I'm a Jew, not a Jew? I'm in the street like another. I went out and was a big parade.

speaker: interviewee
text: If it's true that it was Hitler-- some people say it may not be-- then I've seen Hitler at that time. This was on a Friday, March the 17th, on a parade, from far away, naturally, in a car. Some say it may not have been Hitler, maybe some image. But this history will prove much more. Jews were confined. And Jews were hiding in the houses. Most Jews were wealthy Jews, so they had houses. They had business. From that day on, every day started demonstrations, all kind. Now Jews are not allowed to go to doctors, to non-Jews. Jews are not supposed to have-- and so on. These stories, I'm not going to repeat here. History has all this. What am I doing--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you may be able to be out on the street though? Did you hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was able out there. No one is going to tell me I'm a Jew. I'm not a Jew. I mean, [? I had to. ?] And beside, if someone would find me, I had that I belong to Hungary, because I-- that I, I am from Dolha. But Jews didn't go out so much. And everyone was busy with emigration now. Jewish stores were closed, was written down 'Jude' and every place [INAUDIBLE]. Here comes to my story. It's March. I don't know what's happening there in Hungary. There's no communications. It's not the telephone like here or radios. We only hear the radio was, was the [? there ?] on the German radio.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we see now-- and Brno was-- Germans live there, but never did we see these demonstrations. And everyone appears to be a German now. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And what to do now? So I'm every day looking at it and the demonstrations. They're marching and singing. [INAUDIBLE] [? nice ?] a week or two weeks, and we were announced that the building where we are, the dormitory will be closed. Who is going to taking out? No one knows, but no more dormitory for Jewish and orphans also. Where to go? What to do? We don't know. So we were about five students from the same region. So we rented there a room not far, sleeping five in a room. But it wasn't so bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: The [INAUDIBLE], the free kitchen, still existed. [INAUDIBLE] have to go back to eat at least. Money, we didn't have too much. I sold some clothes. We didn't have [INAUDIBLE]. And so at least there is a place where to go to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: But lines became tremendous big. All those refugees who came from Anschluss from Austria, including those already in Brno, [INAUDIBLE]. And-- but what to do for a meal? You go there where they giving you free. The Jewish community still existed.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're something in a waiting period. There was nothing what to do. In the meantime, we're getting news from Hungary that the students, those who went there, thought they were going to be in Hungary. And they're going to enroll there. They're going to continue. Forget about it. Don't come back to Hungary, they write us. They are sending messages, don't come back. Stay there as long as you can. Because in Hungary, you cannot achieve nothing. They are already there from before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed there. At one point, there's a story that those students who want-- not students-- who wants to go to the [INAUDIBLE], who wants to emigrate to Israel there's a place they call it [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know whether you have-- even know what it means, [INAUDIBLE]. It's a place, if you are a Zionist, and you are going to work for two, three years at a certain place, you'll be able to get a certificate. So the Germans did the following. They needed some work there, and farm [INAUDIBLE]. They said-- they allowed a Jewish organization under the name of [INAUDIBLE] to open a [INAUDIBLE]. They didn't need it. Some farmers, big one or small one, they needed workers. So the Germans said, we'll have workers here from students or from others. And what are we going to do? We allow [INAUDIBLE]. They have never meant to have it. And so I wrote [INAUDIBLE]. And went there and worked there a few days. But I realized that it's only a phase. No one will go [? after ?] Israel. So I left it, and came back to Brno again, and started concentrating what to do next. So I had a passport, which was valid. I figured maybe I'll be able to go still to France. But there is now a story. You need an exit visa. It's not that you have a passport. Passport doesn't mean nothing. With a passport, you just-- you need from the Germans an allowance, regardless who you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there are big and big lines to get it from the Germans, an exit visa. And SS people are there. And Jews didn't dare to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to have to stop here and then pick up where you left off on the next tape. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
